pattern: Scoring the target +1 and everything else -1 reduces majority to a positive subarray sum, and a Fenwick tree counts the strictly-increasing prefix-sum pairs in near-linear time.
hints: Replace each element with +1 if it equals the target and -1 otherwise. The target is the strict majority of a subarray precisely when that subarray's sum is positive.
Using prefix sums P, the subarray [l, r] has positive score when P[r+1] > P[l], so the task is counting pairs i < j with P[j] > P[i].
Sweep the prefix sums left to right; with a Fenwick tree indexed by shifted prefix value, query how many earlier values are strictly less than the current one and add it in.

edge_inputs.description: five subarrays where 2 is the majority
alternating so no window has a strict majority of the target beyond singletons
long run of the target inside noise

stress.description: larger random small-alphabet array to exercise the Fenwick counting at scale
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(100000, size))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 5) for _ in range(n)]
    target = rng.randint(1, 5)
    return [nums, target]
stress.note: n log n approach handles this quickly while the oracle stays quadratic on smaller cases

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def countMajoritySubarrays(self, nums, target):
        n = len(nums)
        offset = n + 1
        size = 2 * n + 3
        bit = [0] * (size + 2)
        def upd(x):
            x += 1
            while x <= size:
                bit[x] += 1
                x += x & -x
        def qry(x):
            x += 1
            s = 0
            while x > 0:
                s += bit[x]
                x -= x & -x
            return s
        ans = 0
        cur = 0
        v = cur + offset
        upd(v)
        for i in range(n):
            cur += 1 if nums[i] == target else -1
            v = cur + offset
            ans += qry(v - 1)
            upd(v)
        return ans
